userStories.title: Diagnose Run 12 and write ANALYSIS.md
userStories.description: Parse episode_summaries.jsonl and produce a comprehensive ANALYSIS.md at the project root. This is the evidence base for all subsequent stories — nothing else should be implemented until this exists.
userStories.hypothesis: We do not yet have a precise, quantified picture of what failed in Run 12. Before changing any code, we need to know: where exactly does q compliance break down by altitude band? Is the 86km plateau a ΔV problem or a reward gradient problem? Does reward increase monotonically with altitude, or is there an anti-incentive similar to Bug 9?
userStories.how_to_analyze: Do NOT read episode_summaries.jsonl directly. Run a Python script via Bash to extract stats and capture the output. Example: use Bash tool with a python3 -c or python3 script that reads the file line by line and computes aggregates. Write the script output into ANALYSIS.md.
what_to_measure: q compliance rate by altitude bucket: episodes where max_q_dyn < 70000, grouped by max_altitude_m into <80km / 80-90km / 90-100km / >100km
mean throttle and mean q_dyn grouped by the same altitude buckets
average reward components (survival, progress, terminal_penalty, terminal_bonus) grouped by max_altitude bucket — check if net reward increases monotonically with altitude
success rate trend: rolling 100-episode window across the full run
curriculum target progression: first/last/min/max of curriculum_target_altitude_m
acceptanceCriteria: ANALYSIS.md exists at project root
Contains quantified q compliance by altitude band (not just overall %)
Contains reward-vs-altitude table showing whether reward is monotonically increasing
Contains at least 3 ranked hypotheses with supporting evidence and confidence levels
Each hypothesis has a proposed experiment and a falsification condition
ANALYSIS.md is committed

userStories.title: Measure and log velocity at burnout per episode
userStories.description: Add velocity_at_burnout_mps to episode_summaries.jsonl. This is a pure observability change — no reward logic touched.
userStories.hypothesis: Mean throttle is 0.249 and burnout altitude is 23.9km. At 24.9% throttle, effective TWR ≈ 0.95g — the rocket is barely climbing. If velocity at burnout is much lower than the ~1,800 m/s needed to coast to 100km, this confirms the policy is leaving ΔV on the table due to over-throttle-suppression from q fear. This measurement will determine whether S4/S5 (throttle shaping) are the right interventions.
implementation_notes: In rl/env.py, track velocity magnitude at the moment burnout_flag transitions to True
Add 'velocity_at_burnout_mps' field to the telemetry dict that gets written to episode_summaries.jsonl
Also add 'vz_at_burnout_mps' (vertical component) — this is what determines apogee height

Verify by running: python3 -c "import json; ep=json.loads(open('artifacts/episode_summaries.jsonl').readlines()[-1]); print(ep.get('velocity_at_burnout_mps', 'MISSING'))" (will show MISSING until next training run, which is expected)
acceptanceCriteria: velocity_at_burnout_mps and vz_at_burnout_mps fields added to episode telemetry in env.py
Fields appear in the episode_summaries.jsonl schema (verify against existing structure)
rl/env.py imports cleanly
Change is committed with a comment explaining the physics: vz_at_burnout determines apogee via vz²/2g

userStories.title: Reduce terminal penalty coefficient to free up reward gradient
userStories.description: The terminal failure penalty averages -57.155 per episode, vs all other reward components combined averaging +28. The penalty is -(50 + 25*altitude_error) where altitude_error = max(0, 1 - alt/target). This means the terminal outcome dominates ~67% of the total reward signal, drowning out the shaping rewards that should guide throttle and q behavior. Reduce the penalty to -(25 + 10*altitude_error).
userStories.hypothesis: When a single reward term dominates the gradient this heavily, the policy optimizes almost entirely for that term and the shaping rewards (progress, q_pen, throttle incentives) become noise. The policy at 86km accepts a -57 terminal penalty on every episode because the shaping rewards can't compensate. Reducing the terminal penalty coefficient will amplify the relative influence of progress and q shaping, allowing the policy to actually learn from altitude and q feedback rather than treating every episode as a binary win/lose.
userStories.causal_chain: terminal_penalty coefficient reduced → terminal outcome's gradient share drops from ~67% to ~40% → progress_reward and q_pen become more influential per episode → policy updates respond more to altitude and q feedback → policy escapes 86km plateau
risks: If reduced too aggressively, policy may not care enough about reaching 100km
Chosen values (25 + 10*err) still make failure strongly negative — just less overwhelming
Should NOT change terminal_bonus (success reward +100) — keep strong positive incentive
implementation_notes: In rl/env.py, find the terminal_penalty calculation
Add a comment: '# Reduced from (50+25*err) — was dominating 67% of gradient, suppressing shaping signals'
Do NOT change the success bonus (+100) or any other reward term in this story
acceptanceCriteria: terminal_penalty formula updated in env.py with explanatory comment
No other reward terms modified in this story
rl/env.py imports cleanly
SCRATCHPAD.md confirms the calculation: at 86km miss (14% error), new penalty = -(25 + 10*0.14*100) = -39 vs old -57
Change committed

userStories.title: Reshape progress potential to have steeper gradient in 80-100km band
userStories.description: The current progress potential phi = clip(alt/target_alt, 0, 2) is linear — every kilometer of altitude gain gives the same reward regardless of how close to the target you are. The policy has settled at 86km, which gives phi=0.86 vs 100km giving phi=1.0. The reward difference per episode between 86km and 100km via progress alone is 4.0 * (1.0 - 0.86) = 0.56. This is a weak signal. Add a bonus multiplier that makes the 80-100km band give steeper rewards.
userStories.hypothesis: The policy is at a local optimum at 86km because the reward gradient is nearly flat near the target. A linear progress reward provides the same per-km incentive at 10km altitude as at 95km altitude. By steepening the gradient in the 80-100km band, we increase the marginal value of each kilometer gained near the goal, giving the policy a stronger pull toward 100km rather than settling 14km short.
userStories.causal_chain: Steeper gradient at 80-100km → each km gain near target worth more in reward → policy's throttle-up behavior in final phase becomes worth the q risk → policy pushes harder to close the 14km gap
userStories.proposed_formula: phi = clip(alt/target_alt, 0, 1.0) + 0.5 * clip((alt - 0.8*target_alt) / (0.2*target_alt), 0, 1.0). This gives: at alt=80km → phi=0.8, at alt=90km → phi=0.95, at alt=100km → phi=1.5. The 80-100km band now yields progress reward of 4.0*(1.5-0.8)=2.8 vs the old 4.0*(1.0-0.8)=0.8 — a 3.5x stronger pull.
risks: If combined with S3's terminal penalty reduction, both changes go in the same direction — do not implement both in the same training run without evaluating S3 first
This story should only be committed AFTER S3 has been validated, OR the user decides to batch both for the next run
Do not change the phi formula for altitudes below 80km — keep the linear region intact so early curriculum stages aren't affected
implementation_notes: In rl/env.py, find _progress_potential() or wherever phi is computed
Replace the existing phi formula with the two-term version above
Add a comment explaining the intent: '# Steeper gradient in 80-100km band to pull policy off 86km plateau'
Verify numerically in SCRATCHPAD.md: compute phi at 50km, 80km, 90km, 95km, 100km with new formula
acceptanceCriteria: Progress potential formula updated in env.py
SCRATCHPAD.md contains the phi values at 50/80/90/95/100km to confirm formula is correct
phi is monotonically increasing from 0 to 100km (verify this)
No other reward terms modified
rl/env.py imports cleanly
Change committed with formula derivation in comment
